title: Psalms 44
subtitle: Why Do You Sleep, O Lord?

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — “In God We Boast All Day Long”

heb.paragraph: "It was not by their sword that they won the land... it was your right arm (zərōʿek̅ā), your arm, and the light of your face" (v.3). A communal lament. The nation remembers God’s past victories: the conquest, the planting, the giving of the land. "Through you we push back our enemies" (v.5). "In God we make our boast all day long" (v.8). The first half is confident.

notes.note: "Our ancestors have told us what you did in their days. With your hand you drove out the nations." Historical memory: what God did before. The recollection prepares for the lament: if you did it then, why not now?
"Through you we push back our enemies. I put no trust in my bow. In God we make our boast." Past faith: the nation trusted God, not weapons.

refs.note: The heroes who conquered kingdoms by faith parallel the psalm's recital of God's mighty acts for the ancestors.
Stephen's speech recounts how God drove out nations before Israel, the same history the psalm celebrates.
Joshua's reminder that victory came not by sword or bow but by God's intervention echoes the psalm's theology.

hist.context: Psalm 44 is a communal lament following military defeat, attributed to the Sons of Korah. The contrast between past victory (vv.1-8) and present disaster (vv.9-16) creates theological tension: what has changed? The community insists on its faithfulness, rejecting the standard explanation that defeat results from covenant violation.

sections.header: Verses 17–26 — “Awake, Lord! Why Do You Sleep?”

heb.paragraph: "Awake (ʿūrâ), Lord! Why do you sleep? Rouse yourself! Do not reject us forever" (v.23). The most daring communal lament. The people insist: "All this came upon us, though we had not forgotten you" (v.17). No sin explains the disaster. "For your sake we face death all day long; we are considered as sheep to be slaughtered" (v.22) — Paul quotes this in Romans 8:36 for the suffering church. The psalm ends with a plea: "Rise up and help us; rescue us because of your unfailing love" (v.26).

notes.note: "For your sake we face death all day long; we are considered as sheep to be slaughtered." Paul quotes this in Romans 8:36 to describe the suffering church. The point: suffering for God’s sake is not a sign of God’s absence.
"Awake, Lord! Why do you sleep?" The boldest possible language. The psalm accuses God of sleeping while his people die. The final appeal is to ḥesed: rescue us because of your unfailing love.

refs.note: Paul quotes verse 22 ('for your sake we face death all day long') to describe the suffering of believers as sheep for slaughter.
Being given over to death for Jesus's sake fulfills the psalm's pattern of innocent suffering.
The disciples' cry 'Don't you care if we drown?' echoes the psalm's 'Awake, Lord! Why do you sleep?'

hist.context: The bold accusation that God sleeps (v.23) pushes covenant language to its limits. Unlike individual laments that confess sin, this communal psalm protests innocence while demanding divine intervention. The sheep-for-slaughter imagery (v.22) became paradigmatic for understanding persecution of the righteous. Paul's citation in Romans 8 interprets Christian suffering through this lens.

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.role: Warrior-God whose past victories make present silence inexplicable (vv.1-8)
ppl.text: The community recalls what 'our ancestors have told us' — God personally drove out nations and planted Israel (vv.1-3). The key theological claim: 'It was not by their sword that they won the land… it was your right hand, your arm, and the light of your face' (v.3). This makes God's current inaction devastating: 'You gave us up like sheep to be devoured' (v.11). Unlike the penitential psalms, the community insists on its innocence (v.17-18), creating the Psalter's starkest protest against undeserved suffering.

ppl.name: The Sons of Korah
ppl.role: Korahite worship guild voicing national lament (superscription)
ppl.text: This corporate lament — 'we' throughout — gives liturgical voice to collective suffering. The Korahites transform battlefield defeat into theological argument: if God fought for Israel before without Israel's merit (vv.1-3), why does he now abandon them despite their loyalty (vv.17-22)? Paul quotes verse 22 ('For your sake we face death all day long; we are considered as sheep to be slaughtered') in Romans 8:36, placing Israel's ancient cry within the church's experience of suffering.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThrough you we push back our enemies; through your name we trample our foes.ESVThrough you we push down our foes; through your name we tread down those who rise up against us.NIVIn God we make our boast all day long, and we will praise your name forever.ESVIn God we have boasted continually, and we will give thanks to your name forever. Selah

rec.text: ""Our ancestors have told us what you did in their days. With your hand you drove out the nations." Historical memory: what God did before. The recollection prepares for the lament: if you did it then,…"

rows.label: Past Victory — 44:1–16
rows.text: "It was your arm, not our sword." Historical memory of God’s conquest.

hebtext.note: "It was not by their sword that they won the land... it was your right arm (zərōʿek̅ā), your arm, and the light of your face" (v.3). A communal lament. The nation remembers God’s past victories: the conquest, the planting, the giving of the land. "Through you we push back our enemies" (v.5). "In God we make our boast all day long" (v.8). The first half is confident.

"Awake (ʿūrâ), Lord! Why do you sleep? Rouse yourself! Do not reject us forever" (v.23). The most daring communal lament. The people insist: "All this came upon us, though we had not forgotten you" (v.17). No sin explains the disaster. "For your sake we face death all day long; we are considered as sheep to be slaughtered" (v.22) — Paul quotes this in Romans 8:36 for the suffering church. The psalm ends with a plea: "Rise up and help us; rescue us because of your unfailing love" (v.26).

themes.note: Justice peaks: innocent communal suffering without explanation. Prophecy: Paul quotes v.22 for the suffering church (Rom 8:36). Sovereignty: the daring "why do you sleep?" assumes God CAN act.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on 'Why do you sleep, O Lord?' consider asking God whether you can worship a God who seems asleep during your suffering — and whether the community's lament gives you permission to voice your own.
